title: Linux & Python Reliability Toolkit
whatIProved: I can build operational monitoring tools, not just follow setup guides.
description: Python-based system monitor that detects failed systemd services, parses journalctl logs for errors, auto-restarts failing services, and fires Slack/email alerts on threshold breach. Runs as a systemd service with structured JSON logging.
operationalBlurb: Owned monitoring daemon: ran 72-hour uptime simulation, validated automated recovery, documented runbook for 3 AM incidents.

quickSummary.problem: No visibility into system health — failed services go unnoticed until users report downtime.
quickSummary.solution: Built a Python monitoring daemon that detects failures, auto-restarts services, and fires webhook alerts with structured JSON logging.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: Tool ran as non-root — restart commands failed silently. Had to rethink privilege model and add startup self-checks.
quickSummary.result: Automated detection + restart with ≤5 min latency. Every failure is logged, alerted, and actionable.

whyItMatters: Unmonitored servers lead to cascading failures. This tool demonstrates building automated health checks that alert engineers before users are affected.

scaling.horizontalScaling: Agent can be deployed on every server via Ansible playbook — stateless design
scaling.capacityNote: Current: single-server monitoring. Target: 50+ servers with centralized Prometheus scraping at 15s intervals.

tradeoffs.alternative: Daemon with inotify for instant detection
tradeoffs.justification: Chose cron to ship a working tool quickly; architecture supports upgrade to daemon in v2.

tradeoffs.choice: Local JSON logs with webhook alerts
tradeoffs.justification: Local logging reduces external dependencies for a single-server tool; centralized logging planned for multi-server scale.

engineeringPractices: Git-based workflow: feature branches with descriptive commit messages
Modular project structure: separate modules for metrics, alerting, service management
Documentation-first: README with setup, usage, architecture, and runbook
Config-driven: all thresholds in config.yaml, zero hardcoded values
interviewBridge: Debugging a silent restart failure due to privilege issues
Cron vs daemon tradeoffs for monitoring latency
Why structured JSON logging matters for log aggregation at scale
highlights: Webhook alerting on CPU >80%, Memory >85%, Disk >90% — thresholds configurable via config.yaml
Service failure detection latency ≤5 min (cron-bounded) or instant when triggered directly
Structured JSON logging — every action and restart attempt logged with timestamp and context
Systemd integration with Restart=on-failure — tool itself is resilient

warStory.title: Service Restart Failed Silently — Wrong User Context
warStory.what: Simulated a service failure by stopping the cron service. The tool detected it correctly and attempted a restart — but the restart silently failed.
warStory.symptoms: Logs showed 'service detected as failed' but the service stayed down. Detection worked. The action didn't. No alert was fired for the failed restart attempt.
warStory.rootCause: The tool was running as a non-root user. systemctl restart requires elevated privileges. The permission error was written to local logs but never surfaced to the alert channel — so it failed invisibly.
warStory.fix: Moved the tool to run as a systemd service under root. Added an explicit privilege check at startup. Surfaced permission errors to the alert webhook instead of only writing them to local logs.
warStory.prevention: Added a startup self-check: tool now verifies it has sufficient privileges to perform restarts before entering the monitoring loop. Fails loudly at startup — not silently at action time.
warStory.impact: Silent restart failure → service stays down → users experience downtime until manual intervention. In production, this could mean minutes to hours of undetected outage.
warStory.customerImpact: Users would experience service downtime without any team being notified — the monitoring tool gave a false sense of security.
runbook: 1. Check tool status: systemctl status reliability-toolkit
2. Review logs: journalctl -u reliability-toolkit --since '30 min ago'
3. Verify permissions: run toolkit with --check-privileges flag
4. Check target service: systemctl status <target-service>
5. Manually restart if needed: sudo systemctl restart <target-service>
6. Confirm recovery: curl health endpoint or check service logs
7. Document in incident log with timestamp and root cause

demo.title: Example Run — Detection + Restart Attempt
demo.content: $ python -m toolkit.main 2026-03-10 04:30:29,566 | INFO | Configuration loaded successfully 2026-03-10 04:30:30,567 | INFO | CPU usage: 0.3% 2026-03-10 04:30:30,568 | INFO | Memory usage: 28.8% 2026-03-10 04:30:30,568 | INFO | Disk usage: 0.4 2026-03-10 04:30:30,573 | WARNING | Service cron is not active. Attempting restart.. 2026-03-10 04:30:30,580 | ERROR | Failed to restart service cron: Permission denied or insufficient privileges 2026-03-10 04:30:30,603 | WARNING | Recent error logs detected

architectureNote: Python daemon managed by systemd with Restart=on-failure. Reads thresholds from config.yaml, probes system via psutil, checks services via systemctl, parses journalctl logs. Alerts via webhook, logs as structured JSON. Startup self-check verifies restart privileges.

title: Bug Tracker — Containerized Stack
whatIProved: I understand container networking, DNS resolution, and load balancing in Docker environments.
description: FastAPI bug/incident tracker containerized with Docker and orchestrated via Docker Compose. Multi-stage builds, custom bridge networking, Nginx reverse proxy, non-root user execution, resource limits, graceful SIGTERM shutdown, and horizontal scaling.
operationalBlurb: Containerized production-grade API: 46% image size reduction, verified load balancing across replicas, documented DNS debugging playbook.

quickSummary.problem: Running multiple services locally leads to port conflicts, dependency hell, and 'works on my machine' issues.
quickSummary.solution: Containerized a full-stack app (FastAPI + Postgres + Nginx) with Docker Compose, custom networking, and multi-stage builds.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: Nginx cached DNS at startup — second replica never received traffic. Required understanding Docker's internal DNS.
quickSummary.result: 46% image size reduction, graceful shutdown in 1–3s, verified load balancing across replicas.

whyItMatters: Most production systems run in containers. This project covers container networking design, image optimization, and real DNS debugging.

scaling.horizontalScaling: API behind Nginx with docker-compose scale — tested with 2 replicas
scaling.capacityNote: Current: 2 replicas handling ~100 RPS. Target: autoscaling group behind ALB with 5+ replicas, PgBouncer connection pooling.

tradeoffs.justification: Chose Alpine for 46% image reduction; CI/CD pull times directly benefit from smaller images.

tradeoffs.decision: Nginx DNS caching vs dynamic resolution
tradeoffs.justification: 5s DNS TTL balances performance with container IP changes; production would use service mesh.
Docker Compose teaches networking fundamentals; Kubernetes adds HPA, PDB, and multi-node — covered in P5.

engineeringPractices: Git-based workflow: feature branches, descriptive commits, PR-based merges
Multi-stage Dockerfiles: build and runtime stages separated
Non-root container execution: security best practice
Environment-based config: .env for secrets, no hardcoded credentials
Health check endpoints: /health returns DB connectivity status
interviewBridge: Debugging Nginx DNS caching in Docker
Multi-stage Docker builds: 46% image reduction explained step by step
Graceful shutdown with SIGTERM and why it matters for zero-downtime deployments
highlights: Image size reduced from 225MB → 120MB (46% reduction) via multi-stage builds + Alpine
Graceful shutdown (SIGTERM) closes DB connections cleanly in 1–3s — vs abrupt SIGKILL risk
Custom bridge network — services resolve each other by name via Docker DNS, not hardcoded IPs
Nginx upstream load balancing across 2 FastAPI replicas with DNS re-resolution

warStory.title: Nginx Upstream Cached Stale DNS — Second Replica Never Received Traffic
warStory.what: Scaled FastAPI to 2 replicas with docker-compose up --scale api=2. Expected Nginx to distribute requests across both containers. All traffic went to one.
warStory.symptoms: Second replica showed zero requests in its access log. Nginx access log showed all upstream hits going to the same IP even after the second container registered on the bridge network.
warStory.rootCause: Nginx resolves upstream DNS at startup and caches it. When the second container came up with a new IP on the bridge network, Nginx was still routing to the original resolved address. Without a resolver directive and the resolve flag, Nginx never re-queries Docker's internal DNS.
warStory.fix: Added resolver 127.0.0.11 valid=5s to the Nginx config and the resolve flag to the upstream server directive. Nginx now re-resolves container IPs every 5 seconds via Docker's embedded DNS server.
warStory.prevention: Added a post-scale verification step to the runbook: after any docker-compose scale, tail both container logs and confirm request distribution before marking the operation successful.
warStory.impact: Uneven traffic distribution → one container overloaded → increased latency and potential OOM kills, while the second replica sits idle. This causes uneven load distribution in real systems.
warStory.customerImpact: Users would experience slower response times during peak traffic because only one server handles all requests instead of two.
runbook: 1. Check container status: docker compose ps
2. Verify both replicas are healthy: docker compose logs api --tail 20
3. Test load distribution: for i in $(seq 1 10); do curl -s localhost:8080/api/whoami; done
4. If traffic is uneven, check Nginx config: docker compose exec nginx cat /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
5. Verify resolver directive: grep 'resolver' nginx.conf
6. Reload Nginx if needed: docker compose exec nginx nginx -s reload
7. Re-test load distribution and confirm even split

demo.title: 3. Failure Handling (DB Down)
demo.content: $ docker compose stop db $ curl http://localhost:8080/api/db-check {"db": "failed"}

architectureNote: All services run on a custom bridge network (app-net). Services resolve each other by name via Docker DNS. Nginx re-resolves upstream IPs every 5s using resolver 127.0.0.11.

title: AWS Production Infrastructure Blueprint
whatIProved: I can provision and manage cloud infrastructure entirely through code.
description: End-to-end AWS infrastructure via Terraform: VPC (10.0.0.0/16), public/private subnets, NAT instance (deliberate cost-learning decision — $8/mo vs $32/mo NAT Gateway), S3 remote state with DynamoDB locking, least-privilege IAM. Configured via Ansible with role-based playbooks, idempotency verified on second run, CloudWatch log shipping.
operationalBlurb: Provisioned 14-resource AWS environment from code: remote state locking, least-privilege IAM, state drift recovery validated end-to-end.

quickSummary.problem: Manual AWS console provisioning is error-prone, unrepeatable, and causes config drift.
quickSummary.solution: Defined entire VPC + EC2 + IAM infrastructure as Terraform code with Ansible for config management.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: Manual console change caused Terraform state drift — had to learn refresh/import workflow.
quickSummary.result: 14 resources fully managed in code. Idempotent Ansible runs. State-locked concurrent applies.

whyItMatters: Infrastructure-as-code with remote state is standard on real teams. This project demonstrates managing AWS resources without the console, handling state drift, and implementing least-privilege IAM. I chose a NAT instance over NAT Gateway to learn networking fundamentals, and I can explain the trade-offs.

scaling.horizontalScaling: App EC2 behind ALB with autoscaling group (design-ready, not yet implemented)
scaling.capacityNote: Current: single EC2 in private subnet. Target: ALB + ASG with min 2 / max 5 instances, target tracking at 70% CPU.

tradeoffs.justification: Chose NAT instance to learn networking fundamentals (source/dest checks, routing, SGs). In production, NAT Gateway is the right answer for HA and zero maintenance.
Self-managed teaches backup, patching, and recovery; production would use RDS for automated failover.
State locking prevents concurrent terraform apply conflicts — essential for team workflows.

engineeringPractices: Infrastructure as Code: zero manual console changes for Terraform-managed resources
Remote state with locking: S3 + DynamoDB for team-safe operations
Least-privilege IAM: only 2 actions granted (S3:GetObject + CloudWatch:PutLogEvents)
Idempotent Ansible: 0 changed tasks on second run — verified
Runbook-documented: incident response steps for state drift recovery
interviewBridge: Recovering from Terraform state drift after manual console changes
NAT instance vs NAT Gateway: cost, manageability, and networking trade-offs
Least-privilege IAM design and why only 2 actions were granted
highlights: 14 AWS resources managed in Terraform state — VPC, subnets, IGW, NAT, EC2, SGs, IAM, route tables
S3 remote backend + DynamoDB locking — concurrent terraform apply conflicts blocked at state level
Least-privilege IAM: 2 actions only — S3:GetObject + CloudWatch:PutLogEvents
Ansible playbook verified idempotent: 0 changed tasks on second run
NAT instance chosen over NAT Gateway — deliberate cost decision ($8/mo vs $32/mo) to learn networking fundamentals

metrics.label: Private Subnet
metrics.note: app EC2 — outbound via NAT only

warStory.title: Manual AWS Console Change Caused Terraform State Drift
warStory.what: Deleted a security group rule manually in the AWS console to test something quickly. Forgot to revert it. The next terraform plan showed unexpected resource recreation.
warStory.symptoms: terraform plan wanted to recreate a resource that appeared to already exist. The diff was confusing — the Terraform config was unchanged, but plan showed a destructive action.
warStory.rootCause: Terraform state held the original configuration. The manual console change caused drift — real AWS state diverged from Terraform state. Terraform planned to restore the resource to match its state file, not to match what was actually in AWS.
warStory.fix: Ran terraform refresh to sync state with actual AWS reality. Then terraform apply to restore the resource to the intended configuration. Total recovery: under 5 minutes once root cause was identified.
warStory.prevention: Added a hard rule to the runbook: never touch AWS console for Terraform-managed resources. Any emergency manual change must be immediately followed by terraform import or refresh + a written incident note in the repo.
warStory.impact: State drift → unexpected resource recreation on next apply → potential production downtime if a security group or route table is destroyed and recreated.
warStory.customerImpact: If this happened in production, recreating a security group could temporarily expose or block traffic to application servers.
runbook: 1. Check state drift: terraform plan (look for unexpected changes)
2. If drift detected: terraform refresh to sync with real AWS
3. Review refreshed plan: terraform plan (should now show only intended changes)
4. Apply carefully: terraform apply with -target if needed
5. If resource was manually created: terraform import <resource> <aws-id>
6. Verify: aws ec2 describe-security-groups / describe-instances
7. Document incident: git commit with note explaining what drifted and why

architectureNote: VPC: 10.0.0.0/16 · Public subnet (10.0.1.0/24): bastion + NAT · Private subnet (10.0.2.0/24): app EC2, no direct internet route. Terraform remote state in S3 + DynamoDB lock. 14 resources managed. NAT instance chosen deliberately over NAT Gateway ($8/mo vs $32/mo).

title: Secure CI/CD Pipeline
whatIProved: Automated deployment pipeline with security scanning, blue/green rollout, and auto-rollback.
description: GitHub Actions pipeline: lint → test → build → Trivy CVE scan → push to ECR → deploy to EC2 → ALB health check → auto-rollback on failure. Blue/green deployment via ALB target group switching.

quickSummary.problem: Manual deployments are slow, risky, and lack security scanning.
quickSummary.solution: Full CI/CD pipeline with automated security scanning, blue/green deployment, and auto-rollback.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: Balancing pipeline speed with thorough security scanning.
quickSummary.result: Zero-downtime deployment with every artifact traceable to a git commit and CVE-scanned.

whyItMatters: CI/CD is the backbone of modern software delivery. This project covers building a pipeline that ships fast and safely, with rollback, traceability, and security gates.
highlights: Trivy blocks pipeline on CRITICAL CVEs — no image reaches prod without a clean scan
Auto-rollback: previous git SHA image re-deployed if health check fails post-deploy
Blue/green via ALB target group swap — zero-downtime deployment verified end-to-end
Git SHA used as image tag — every deployed artifact is traceable to a specific commit

title: Kubernetes + War Room Stack
whatIProved: Production-grade orchestration with real incident response and chaos testing.
description: Full Kubernetes deployment with HPA, PDB, NetworkPolicy, RBAC, PVC. Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager via Helm. 4 chaos scenarios with structured incident reports.

quickSummary.problem: Containers alone don't handle scaling, self-healing, or incident response.
quickSummary.solution: Kubernetes with HPA, chaos engineering, and full observability stack.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: Executing chaos scenarios and documenting structured incident reports.
quickSummary.result: Auto-scaling, zero-downtime drains, network segmentation, 4 documented chaos war stories.

whyItMatters: Kubernetes is where DevOps interviews get challenging. This project covers production-grade orchestration with real incident response.
highlights: 4 chaos scenarios: pod crash, CPU spike, network partition, node drain — all documented
HPA: pods scale from 1 to 5 at 60% CPU utilization
NetworkPolicy: only API pods allowed to reach DB — zero lateral movement
PodDisruptionBudget: minimum 1 pod always available during node drain

title: MLOps Production Pipeline
whatIProved: ML model serving as a production service with versioning, monitoring, and rollback.
description: FastAPI serving a Hugging Face model with P99 latency histogram, blue/green rollout, auto-rollback on latency spike, model versioning via S3.

quickSummary.problem: ML models in notebooks don't survive production.
quickSummary.solution: Versioned Kubernetes service with monitoring and rollback.
quickSummary.result: Production-grade ML serving with versioning and cost estimation.

whyItMatters: MLOps demonstrates treating ML models as production services, not just notebooks.
highlights: Model artifacts versioned in S3
P99 latency as Prometheus histogram
Auto-rollback on 3x baseline spike
Cost estimation per instance type

title: LLM / RAG Infrastructure
whatIProved: AI infrastructure with cost visibility, rate limiting, and secret management.
description: RAG pipeline with per-request cost tracking, Redis caching, rate limiting, API key rotation without pod restart.

quickSummary.problem: LLM APIs are expensive and uncontrolled.
quickSummary.solution: Per-query cost tracking, Redis caching, rate limiting.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: API key rotation without pod restart.
quickSummary.result: Cost visibility, caching, rate limiting, zero-downtime secret rotation.

whyItMatters: Shows thinking about AI infrastructure as a business problem, not just a technical one.
highlights: Per-query cost tracking
Redis cache: zero LLM cost on repeat queries
Rate limiter: 10 req/min with 429 + Retry-After
K8s Secret rotation without pod restart

title: AI-Augmented SRE Dashboard
whatIProved: AI-assisted operations with confidence scoring and human approval gates.
description: AlertManager webhook → LLM root-cause analysis with confidence scores → human approval gate. Zero automation without explicit consent.

quickSummary.problem: On-call alert fatigue — too many alerts, not enough context.
quickSummary.solution: AI-assisted triage with confidence scores and human approval gate.
quickSummary.keyChallenge: Building trust: confidence scoring and mandatory verification warnings.
quickSummary.result: Structured triage, immutable audit trail, zero automation without consent.

whyItMatters: AI in operations is growing, but trust and safety are the bottleneck.
highlights: Confidence scoring: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
Immutable S3 audit trail
Human approval gate
Documented limitations
